title: Coulterina newberryi
intro: Coulterina newberryi, also known as Newberry's Coulterina or Coulter's Coulterina, is a perennial herbaceous plant native to the Pacific Northwest region of North America. It has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, which can aid in the treatment of wounds, infections, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Coulterina newberryi include phenolic acids, flavonoids, and volatile oils, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Coulterina newberryi include teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are often used to soothe coughs, reduce inflammation, and promote wound healing.
taxonomy: Coulterina newberryi is classified within the plant family Apiaceae, commonly known as the carrot or parsley family. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics and morphological features. Coulterina newberryi is further classified as a genus within the tribe Smeilacinae. The tribe Smeilacinae is a group of flowering plants that are native to North America. Coulterina newberryi is a unique species within this classification, sharing characteristics with other plants in the same tribe.
